posts.text:
Organizations aren't monolithic. They're arenas of competing stakeholder goals. Yet most management models treat them as unified entities.

Reality? Teams form factions—subcoalitions—with aligned preferences. These fault lines drive decision-making chaos.

The problem? Subcoalitions are hard to observe and harder to model. Machine learning struggles with incomplete data and group dynamics complexity.

Enter SCA: A computational breakthrough that maps hidden organizational fault lines.

How does SCA work? Think preference-based clustering that groups actors by maximizing shared utility.

✅ Initialization: Seeds meaningful coalitions
✅ Renegotiation: Fine-tunes alignment
✅ Stability: Stops when no one wants to defect

Case study: Wikipedia's 2013 Visual Editor rollout sparked rebellion from veteran editors.

SCA revealed two warring subcoalitions:
🛠️ Technical design focus
🌍 Cultural norms focus

Leadership missed these divisions—leading to strategy disaster.

Baseball Hall of Fame voting (2009-2016): Writers debated inducting players linked to PEDs.

SCA uncovered the rift:
⚾ Pro-PED forgiveness
⚾ Anti-PED purists

This schism grew, reinforcing decision-making silos over time.

SCA turns organizational chaos into clarity. Results suggest managers can:

📊 Identify internal conflicts before they explode
🎯 Tailor strategies to real divisions
⚡ Avoid paralysis from hidden factional wars

But it's not just theory. SCA is computationally practical:
💻 Handles incomplete data
📈 Adapts across industries
⚡ Fast enough for real-world applications

What subcoalitions are hiding in your workplace?

For researchers and org leaders studying conflict dynamics—this method offers new insights into how factions form and persist.

Paper: [LINK]

[Add coauthors]

#AIGovernance #ResponsibleAI #BehavioralTheory #ConflictResolution

linkedin.text:
Ever felt like your organization is a battlefield of hidden alliances and clashing interests? New research introduces Subcoalition Cluster Analysis (SCA)—a computational method to uncover the invisible factions driving organizational conflict. [LINK]

Organizations aren't monolithic entities. They're arenas of competing stakeholder goals where teams form factions—subcoalitions—with aligned preferences. These fault lines drive decision-making chaos, yet most management models treat organizations as unified.

➤ The breakthrough: SCA maps hidden organizational divisions using preference-based clustering that groups actors by maximizing shared utility

➤ Real-world validation: Wikipedia's 2013 Visual Editor rollout revealed two warring subcoalitions (technical vs. cultural focus), explaining why the initiative sparked rebellion from veteran editors

➤ Practical applications: Baseball Hall of Fame voting analysis (2009-2016) uncovered persistent rifts between pro-PED forgiveness and anti-PED purist factions

Results suggest SCA can help managers identify internal conflicts before they explode, tailor strategies to real divisions rather than assumptions, and avoid decision paralysis caused by hidden factional wars.

The method is computationally practical—handling incomplete data, adapting across industries, and running fast enough for real-world applications.

For researchers and organizational leaders studying conflict dynamics, this offers new insights into how factions form and persist within institutions.

[Add coauthors] | [Add funder]

GRAIL Center | Purdue University

#AIGovernance #ResponsibleAI #BehavioralTheory #ConflictResolution #OrganizationalPsychology #DataScience
